elements.sent-2411-001: Multimodal foundation models (MFMs) represent a significant advancement in artificial intelligence, combining diverse data modalities to enhance learning and understanding across a wide range of applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, this integration also brings unique safety and security challenges.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we conceptualize cybersafety and cybersecurity in the context of multimodal learning and present a comprehensive Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) to unify these concepts in MFMs, identifying key threats to these models.
elements.sent-2411-004: We propose a taxonomy framework grounded in information theory, evaluating and categorizing threats through the concepts of channel capacity, signal, noise, and bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-005: This approach provides a novel framework that unifies model safety and system security in MFMs, offering a more comprehensive and actionable understanding of the risks involved.
elements.sent-2411-006: We used this to explore existing defense mechanisms, and identified gaps in current research -- particularly, a lack of protection for alignment between modalities and a need for more systematic defense methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the security and safety landscape in MFMs, providing researchers and practitioners with valuable insights for improving the robustness and reliability of these models.

elements.sent-2411-008: Multimodal foundation models (MFMs) are a form of artificial intelligence (AI) whichintegrate diverse data types, such as text, images, and audio, into unified frameworks capable of adapting to numerous downstream tasks~\cite{fei2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Notable examples of large-scale MFMs include the feature alignment model OpenAI's CLIP~\cite{radford2021learning , image-generating diffusion models~\cite{rombach2022high , and multimodal large language models such as GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Deployed in industries such as healthcare, autonomous systems, content creation, MFMs can solve tasks beyond the reach of simpler unimodal models.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, their rapid integration into high-stakes environments, including autonomous vehicles and medical diagnostics, amplifies the need for robust safety and security measures~\cite{KDD2024Zhao,han2022multimodal .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, this growing use of MFMs in critical systems provides two main concerns: ensuring reliable, harm-free performance (safety) and protecting against malicious attacks (security).
elements.sent-2411-013: These issues, traditionally treated separately, are increasingly intertwined~\cite{arxiv2024Qi .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Attacks on MFMs often undermine both safety and security, highlighting the need for unified analysis.
elements.sent-2411-015: In addition, because of the richer data forms processed by MFMs, a wider range of more complex and covert attack vectors and scenarios have emerged as compared with simpler unimodal models.
elements.sent-2411-016: This motivates our SoK paper.. Our approach.
elements.sent-2411-017: To address the challenge of unifying safety and security in MFMs, we propose an innovative approach grounded in information theory.
elements.sent-2411-018: Information theory offers a powerful framework for understanding how information is transmitted, processed, and integrated across multiple modalities.
elements.sent-2411-019: Specifically, we utilize concepts such as channel capacity, bandwidth, signal, and noise.
elements.sent-2411-020: At the model level, our focus is on identifying safety threats that manipulate or exploit model vulnerabilities, either by diminishing the effective signal input to the system or by amplifying the disruptive impact of noise.
elements.sent-2411-021: At the system level, we extend this analysis by considering bandwidth constraints and exploring how information flows between agents and components across the broader system.
elements.sent-2411-022: This integrated approach offers a comprehensive framework for understanding and mitigating threats in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-023: Additionally, we examine existing defense mechanisms, assess their effectiveness through experiments and analysis, and propose future research directions to further enhance the resilience of multimodal systems.
elements.sent-2411-024: An overview of our SoK is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig_overview .}
elements.sent-2411-026: A recent study by Qi~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Qi identifies inconsistencies in the definitions of ``safety'' and ``security'' across various research communities and proposes a framework to clarify the distinctions and interactions between AI safety and AI security.}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, their work remains high-level and does not address the specific technical challenges posed by MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-028: Other studies focus specifically on large language models (LLMs) and their vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-029: For instance, Shayegani~\etal~\cite{arxiv2023Shayegani and Das~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Das investigate the security and privacy challenges associated with emerging attack vectors in LLMs, while Wu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Wu extend this analysis to real-world LLM-based systems.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Several surveys~\cite{arXiv2024Yi,CCS2024Shen highlight the limitations of existing defenses, though they primarily focus on a narrow subset of security threats, such as jailbreaking attacks, rather than addressing the broader threat landscape.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the multimodal domain, Fan~\etal~\cite{ICSMC2024Fan examine vulnerabilities introduced by image inputs in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), and Liu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Liu2 review a range of attack methods, including adversarial, jailbreak, and prompt injection attacks on large vision-language models (LVLMs).}}
elements.sent-2411-032: While these studies focus on individual modalities or model-level threats, our work aims to provide a comprehensive review that unifies safety and security concerns at both the model and system levels for MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-033: We detail the literature collation method in Appendix~\ref{apdx_literature_collection .. Contributions.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We present a comprehensive review of \nPapersRemain recent studies on the safety and security of MFMs, exploring how different modalities and information flows introduce unique vulnerabilities and challenges in MFM systems.
elements.sent-2411-037: Unified framework for threat analysis using information theory.
elements.sent-2411-038: We propose a novel threat taxonomy framework grounded in information theory, particularly adapting the Shannon–Hartley theorem~\cite{taub1986principles,ziemer2014principles to the context of multimodal learning.}
elements.sent-2411-039: This framework unifies analysis of safety (focusing on the signal-to-noise balance) and security (emphasizing bandwidth constraints), and how these factors interact in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-040: Furthermore, we identified 6 exploitable information flows encoded by the information theory: prediction, learning, and inference at the model level, as well as information flows between agents and applications, multi-agent interactions, and system memory at the system level.
elements.sent-2411-041: These let us produce a taxonomy to group attacks depending on their targeted information flows.
elements.sent-2411-042: We categorized \nModelAttacks model-level safety attacks and \nSystemAttacks system-level security attacks based on this framework.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our findings reveal that attacks targeting multiple modalities simultaneously can be carried out more subtly and with greater ease.
elements.sent-2411-044: An evaluation of defensive and mitigation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-045: We examine existing MFM defenses and attack mitigation strategies, providing an experimental analysis over multiple methods.
elements.sent-2411-046: The results show that while defenses mitigate some of the impact of attacks, they remain inadequate and come with significant costs, achieving at most 51.8\% accuracy on adversarial samples when against co-modality misleading attacks, and demonstrating insufficient performance against mislearning attacks.
elements.sent-2411-047: This suggests that relying solely on model-level protection is ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-049: We propose several directions for future research.
elements.sent-2411-050: We also emphasize the importance of understanding system-level interactions and propose new avenues for investigating cross-modal vulnerabilities in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-051: The unique architecture of multimodal models, especially their feature alignment and aggregation mechanisms, increases the potential for subtle, model-level attacks.
elements.sent-2411-052: This underscores the need for stronger protection measures and effective oversight.
elements.sent-2411-053: However, existing defenses often target specific vulnerable information flows or single modalities.
elements.sent-2411-054: We argue that applying bandwidth constraints at the system level offers considerable advantages and relying solely on model-level protection is often insufficient and sometimes ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this section, we first explore how multimodal learning has evolved from unimodal learning and then introduce several typical MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-057: Multimodal learning extends beyond traditional unimodal learning by integrating data from various modalities like text, images, and audio.
elements.sent-2411-058: In unimodal learning (Figure~\ref{fig_single_multi (a)), the model maps input features to output labels within a discrete feature space, focusing on patterns within one data type.}
elements.sent-2411-059: It extracts features, converts them into vectors, and then predicts labels based on these vectors.
elements.sent-2411-060: In contrast, multimodal learning (Figure~\ref{fig_single_multi (b)) involves mapping and aligning continuous feature spaces across different modalities, such as pairing images with text.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Instead of directly predicting labels, multimodal models create unified representations by linking feature spaces from different data types.
elements.sent-2411-062: This allows for more complex tasks as the model can work with more diverse information.. Existing MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-063: Within the image/text multimodal space, there are four typical classes of multimodal foundation models (MFMs): feature alignment models, text-to-image generation models, audio-to-text generation models, and models that take both text and image inputs, as summarized in Table~\ref{tab_mlm_models .}

elements.sent-2411-066: It trains on large image-text pairs using contrastive learning~\cite{chen2020simple to create a shared representation space, excelling in tasks like zero-shot classification and image retrieval.}
elements.sent-2411-068: DALL·E~\cite{ramesh2021zero and Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high are prominent models in this category, generating images by embedding text prompts into models.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: These models typically leverage feature alignment techniques, such as CLIP, to convert text into feature vectors that serve as conditional guidance for image generation, enabling the model to synthesize visuals corresponding to the input text.

elements.sent-2411-075: In this section, we first outline the safety and security objectives in MFMs, then propose an information theory-based framework to analyze the interactions between these two concepts within MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-076: Across a broad range of sectors (e.g. aviation, nuclear energy, chemistry, power grids, and information systems), safety and security have conventionally been distinguished by intentionality~\cite{pietre2010sema,hansson2013ethics,ale2009risk,firesmith2003common,porzsolt2011safety .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Safety focuses on preventing unintentional accidents, while security addresses protection from deliberate malicious actions~\cite{arxiv2024Qi .}
elements.sent-2411-078: In multimodal learning, safety refers to the system’s capacity to operate as intended without causing harm, even when faced with unpredictable conditions or inputs.
elements.sent-2411-079: For example, a vision-language model in an autonomous vehicle must not misinterpret shadows as obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-080: Security, on the other hand, involves defending against adversarial attacks and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-081: Adversarial examples can subtly alter inputs, misleading models into incorrect predictions, such as small image perturbations generating misleading captions.
elements.sent-2411-082: Security also involves preventing data breaches that expose or manipulate sensitive information (\eg facial recognition).
elements.sent-2411-084: This distinction between safety and security, however, can sometimes lead to confusion in the security domain.
elements.sent-2411-085: It is not entirely appropriate to differentiate between safety and security threats based solely on the presence of malicious intent, as all attack threat models inherently involve adversaries.
elements.sent-2411-086: For example, many attacks traditionally classified as safety attacks, such as jailbreak~\cite{NeurIPS2023Carlini,AAAI2024Qi or latency attacks~\cite{ICLR2024Gao,arxiv2023Baras , involve adversarial intents and should be recognized as security-related according to this definition.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Furthermore, the concept of model safety also encompasses objectives like adversarial robustness~\cite{NDSS2018Xu,ICLR2018Guo , uncertainty estimation, trojan detection~\cite{arxiv2023Zhang2 , and alignment with human values~\cite{amodei2016concrete , which overlap with the root causes of security issues.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: At this level, we argue that security threats essentially represent attacks that target the model’s safety objectives.
elements.sent-2411-089: Thus, these model-level threats should be seen as safety threats.
elements.sent-2411-090: In practice, addressing safety issues at the model level involves tackling vulnerabilities throughout the model's entire lifecycle, from training to deployment and inference.
elements.sent-2411-092: AI agents have been embedded in web platforms, email systems, file management services, and many other applications.
elements.sent-2411-093: At system levels such as these, security threats often arise and cannot be fully understood or mitigated by focusing solely on the model itself.
elements.sent-2411-094: For example, indirect prompt injection attacks~\cite{CCSW2023Greshake,arxiv2024Wu4 can place malicious prompts in external sources retrieved at inference time, such as in emails or web pages~\cite{arxiv2024Wu5,arxiv2024Wu4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-095: This enables attackers to manipulate an AI agent into unknowingly executing a malicious prompt, potentially exposing sensitive information to unauthorized third parties.
elements.sent-2411-096: In such cases, even if each component in the system maintains good safety, without threats identification or applying operation constraints at the system level, serious security breaches can still occur within the broader system.
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, we emphasize that security issues must be addressed at the system level, where interactions between various components (agents, applications, and system memory) can provide for new risks and vulnerabilities.
elements.subsection-2411-002: An Information Processing Model to Unify Security and Safety in MFMs
elements.sent-2411-098: Considering that machine learning models act as channels for information communication, where information flows from input samples through the model and eventually to the outputs, further spreading to other components in a system, we argue that information theory offers a suitable framework for analyzing the transmission, processing, and fusion of information across multimodal models.
elements.sent-2411-099: Specifically, we adapt the Shannon–Hartley theorem~\cite{taub1986principles,ziemer2014principles to multimodal safety and security analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-100: This has the formula: \begin{equation}\label{eq_sh_theorem} C=B\log_2 (1+\frac{S}{N}),\end{equation} where $C$ is the channel capacity (the measure of information transfer), $B$ is the bandwidth of the channel (the availability for transmission), $S$ is the signal power (the meaningful information being transmitted), $N$ is the noise power (the unwanted interference affecting the signal).
elements.sent-2411-101: For multimodal learning, we can adapt these definitions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-102: Channel capacity represents the model's ability to effectively learn from and predict based on multimodal inputs, which is essential for assessing how well the model transmits meaningful information.
elements.sent-2411-103: Signal refers to the meaningful information that can be extracted from various modalities, such as textual features from documents, visual features from images, or auditory features from sound clips.
elements.sent-2411-104: Noise refers to any irrelevant or disruptive information that can distort the intended signal, which can stem from sources such as data collection errors, sensor inaccuracies, or deliberate perturbations injected into different modalities.
elements.sent-2411-105: Bandwidth refers to the capacity for transmitting information between agents or system components, determining how information can be exchanged, which directly impacts the performance and responsiveness of each agent within the system.
elements.sent-2411-106: At the model level, improving performance and ensuring accurate predictions requires maximizing the effective signal.
elements.sent-2411-107: Robust models must accurately interpret and integrate data from various modalities to avoid errors or harmful outputs.
elements.sent-2411-108: Reducing noise, whether through data preprocessing, feature selection, or other methods, helps preserve relevant information while minimizing disruptive factors, leading to better model performance and safer operation.
elements.sent-2411-109: From an information theory perspective, this can be understood through Equation~\ref{eq_sh_theorem , where safety-focused approaches aim to increase the signal-to-noise ratio ($S/N$).}
elements.sent-2411-110: In contrast, safety attacks work to decrease this ratio and distort the information received by the model, either in training or prediction stages.
elements.sent-2411-111: At the system level, information transmission considerations are critical in multimodal learning.
elements.sent-2411-112: Bandwidth~$B$ in Equation~\ref{eq_sh_theorem influences the flow of information between different applications and agents, directly affecting their ability to process and combine diverse data types.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Previous research~\cite{arxiv2024Wu4 has shown that vulnerabilities may not be apparent when evaluating a single agent in isolation (\eg robust safety control $S/N$ at model level); however, attacks can still exploit interactions across multiple agents, leading to significant security breaches within the system due to poor security management on $B$.}
elements.sent-2411-114: To safeguard against security threats, it is essential to establish bandwidth constraints, including restrictions on agent behavior and filters that control the flow of information between agents.
elements.sent-2411-115: These measures are crucial for maintaining security at the system level.
elements.sent-2411-116: Information theory provides the necessary tools to analyze and optimize the interactions among signals, bandwidth, and noise, guiding us in creating resilient mechanisms that bolster both safety and security.
elements.sent-2411-117: In this section, we introduce safety and security threats within a unified threat model, categorizing them based on the adversary’s goals, knowledge, and capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-118: We also outline the information flows in MFMs to highlight specific points of vulnerability that adversaries may target.
elements.sent-2411-119: At the model level, attackers aim to compromise the model by either forcing it to produce incorrect results or by extracting unauthorized information from its output:.
elements.sent-2411-121: A key goal is to degrade model performance through adversarial examples, leading to unreliable predictions by manipulating the decision-making process across modalities.
elements.sent-2411-123: Introducing malicious data into the training set to corrupt the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-124: This can degrade performance or alter behavior to serve the attacker's interests, posing significant security risks.
elements.sent-2411-126: Hidden triggers are introduced during training, allowing attackers to activate malicious behaviors under specific conditions, compromising model integrity.
elements.sent-2411-128: Attackers may bypass safety mechanisms by exploiting model weaknesses, enabling the generation of unrestricted or harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-130: Attacks may increase latency or energy consumption, acting as denial-of-service tactics or exposing inefficiencies, disrupting service availability or inflating operational costs.
elements.sent-2411-132: Adversaries may seek to extract proprietary prompts by analyzing input-output pairs, potentially enabling unauthorized access to model capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-134: Using targeted techniques to infer training data, potentially exposing sensitive information or intellectual property.
elements.sent-2411-136: Determining whether specific data points were part of the model’s training set, compromising privacy and confidentiality.
elements.sent-2411-137: At the system level, attackers aim to manipulate the system by misleading MFMs into performing unexpected behaviors, harmful interactions, or triggering malicious payloads:.
elements.sent-2411-139: Forcing agents to act contrary to their intended purpose, leading to dangerous actions or the spread of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-141: Redirecting the agent's objectives to serve the attacker’s intentions, hijacking the decision-making process for malicious activities.
elements.sent-2411-143: Forcing agents to visit malicious or illegal web links or images, leading to security breaches or exploitation of vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-145: Generating harmful code that compromises system integrity, spreads malware, or exploits weaknesses in connected systems.
elements.sent-2411-147: Spreading false or manipulated content that misguides other agents or users, impacting decision-making processes across systems.
elements.sent-2411-149: Extracting sensitive information by revealing hidden prompts or internal instructions, compromising user privacy and system security.
elements.sent-2411-150: Some model-level goals, such as backdoor insertion, jailbreak, privacy leakage, data extraction, and membership inference, can also be achieved at the system level.
elements.sent-2411-151: Based on the level of accessibility, an attacker's knowledge can be categorized into three main types: white-box, black-box, and grey-box attacks.
elements.sent-2411-152: White-box attacks (\eg~\cite{ICCVW2023Schlarmann,NeurIPS2023Carlini ) assume the adversary has comprehensive access to the MFM system's data, covering both training and inference phases.}
elements.sent-2411-153: In training, attackers can access the dataset, pre-trained models, MFM architecture, and hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-154: During inference, their knowledge extends to the trained model's architecture and additional functions used in the MFM system, such as pre- or post-processing functions and defense mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-155: While this level of access is less practical and typically applies only to open-source models, it serves as a foundation for advanced attacks.
elements.sent-2411-156: Black-box attacks (\eg~\cite{NeurIPS2023Yin,arxiv2023Gong ) assume attackers have limited access to the MFM system, typically lacking insight into the training process, datasets, and hyperparameters.}
elements.sent-2411-157: However, they can observe inputs and outputs during inference.
elements.sent-2411-158: Attackers may use auxiliary knowledge based on the MFM’s intended task to gather relevant data samples and construct shadow datasets that closely mimic training and inference data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-159: Grey-box attacks (\eg~\cite{NeurIPS2022Hu,arXiv2024Zhai ) fall between white-box and black-box scenarios, where attackers have partial knowledge of the MFM system.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Some knowledge, such as the encoder used for specific modalities or general architectural components, may be readily accessible and common across different MFM systems.
elements.sent-2411-161: Grey-box attacks also consider the possibility that attackers may be aware of well-known defense mechanisms employed by the system, such as filters or detectors, which serve as additional functional blocks within the MFM architecture.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{figs/information_flow.pdf} \caption{An illustration of information flows in MFM system (represented by arrows).} \label{fig_information_flow}
elements.sent-2411-162: Multimodal learning presents distinct challenges and vulnerabilities compared to unimodal systems, which manifest through various information flows within the model, as well as interactions between models (as shown in Figure~\ref{fig_information_flow ).}
elements.sent-2411-163: Here, we categorize these flows and the associated adversary's capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-164: At the model level, the information flows include prediction, learning, and inferring.
elements.sent-2411-165: Prediction information flow (\eg~\cite{MM2022Zhang,NeurIPS2023Yin ) involves feeding multimodal inputs into a trained model, to generate predictions or outputs, where the inputs are digitized and encoded into latent space representations However, adversarial attacks can introduce malicious inputs that deceive the model, leading to incorrect or biased results.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Learning information flow (\eg~\cite{Oakland2024Shan ) refers to the process of training or fine-tuning a multimodal model using input from a training dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-167: An attacker may inject incorrect or malicious samples into the training dataset, causing the model to mislearn and resulting in incorrect predictions or behavior.
elements.sent-2411-168: Inferring information flow (\eg~\cite{ICCV2023Ko,USENIX2023Carlini ) refers to the path of information in certain attacks where attackers can reverse-engineer model outputs to infer sensitive data or use malicious queries to extract confidential information.}
elements.sent-2411-169: From the system perspective, the information flows between various components, such as models, databases, and applications, can be categorized as:.
elements.sent-2411-170: Information flow between agents and applications (\eg~\cite{NeurIPSW2022Perez,arxiv2023Pedro ) refers to how agents within a system carry out actions based on the model’s outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Attackers may exploit this flow to misdirect the agent’s actions, causing unintended or harmful behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-172: Information flow between multi-agents (\eg~\cite{arxiv2024Tan, ACSAC2023Weeks ) involves the communication and coordination between agents within a system.}
elements.sent-2411-173: In multi-agent environments, adversaries can exploit this interaction by injecting false information or instructions that propagate throughout the system, disrupting coordination and enabling harmful behaviors to spread across otherwise secure agents.
elements.sent-2411-174: Information flow between agent and system memory (\eg~\cite{EMNLP2023Zhong, arxiv2024Zeng3 ) refers to how agents store, retrieve, and rely on historical data for decision-making.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Attackers may target the integrity of this memory by manipulating stored information or introducing false data.
elements.sent-2411-176: Traditionally, safety risks and security attacks have been categorized based on their observable effects rather than the underlying technical vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-177: Terms like ``adversarial attack'', ``jailbreak attack'', and ``membership inference attack'' focus on the outcomes of these threats but do not directly express the root causes in the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-178: We argue that categorizing these risks based on the underlying vulnerable points offers clearer insights into how the learning process is exploited.
elements.sent-2411-179: Specifically, we propose classifying threats in MFMs according to the information flows they target: prediction, learning, and inferring for model safety threats, and agent action, interaction, and memory flows for system security.
elements.sent-2411-180: This unifies safety and security concerns under a single framework.

elements.sent-2411-181: In this section, we introduce a taxonomy of model-level safety threats in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-182: We categorize safety attacks against MFMs into three types: input misleading, model mislearning, and output inferring attacks, each addressing specific vulnerable information flows within the multimodal models.
elements.sent-2411-183: We present the taxonomy of safety threats in Figure~\ref{fig_tax_safety and summarize related studies in Table~\ref{tab_big_table .}}

elements.sent-2411-184: Misleading attacks aim to intentionally deceive multimodal models during their inference processes, resulting in incorrect or manipulated predictions.
elements.sent-2411-185: These attacks exploit weaknesses in the model’s decision-making by providing carefully crafted inputs designed to confuse or misdirect the model.
elements.sent-2411-186: They can be executed through various methods, including adversarial examples, prompt manipulations, or subtle data alterations that influence how the model interprets multimodal inputs.
elements.sent-2411-187: A misleading attack exploits inherent errors in machine learning caused by discrepancies between the training samples and ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-188: By manipulating the inputs, the attackers push adversarial samples into this error zone.
elements.sent-2411-189: Figure~\ref{fig_misleading in the Appendix shows an example of misleading attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-190: Several recent studies have explored various types of misleading attacks, which can be categorized into two main approaches: perturbation-based and structure-based attacks.
elements.sent-2411-191: From an information theory perspective, perturbation-based attacks aim to increase the noise $N$ in the system, effectively degrading the channel capacity $C$ and reducing the model’s performance by distorting the input signal.
elements.sent-2411-192: This added perturbation leads to unreliable or erroneous predictions by disrupting the clarity of the input.
elements.sent-2411-193: Structure-based attacks, on the other hand, manipulate the signal $S$ itself, such as embedding text signals into images, exploiting differences in how models process various modalities.
elements.sent-2411-194: For example, a model’s safety alignment mechanism may be sensitive to text inputs but less effective at detecting malicious content in images, even when both convey the same information.
elements.sent-2411-195: The actual $S$ recognized by the image modality could be smaller than through text, further reducing the channel capacity $C$.
elements.sent-2411-196: Both types of attacks are covert, designed to mislead the model during inference while avoiding detection.. Perturbation-based attacks.
elements.sent-2411-197: These attacks aim to induce incorrect predictions, which adversaries can exploit, raising significant security concerns.
elements.sent-2411-198: For instance, VLAttack~\cite{NeurIPS2023Yin generates perturbations on images and, if ineffective, shifts to text.}
elements.sent-2411-199: This method leverages pre-trained vision-language models to enhance adversarial transferability across modalities.
elements.sent-2411-200: Schlarmann~\etal~\cite{ICCVW2023Schlarmann introduce a white-box attack on images to manipulate captions generated by multimodal models.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Zhao~\etal~\cite{NeurIPS2023Zhao focus on the most vulnerable modality, \eg images, targeting pretrained models like CLIP and BLIP.}
elements.sent-2411-202: These adversarial examples are transferable across VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-203: Dong~\etal~\cite{NeurIPSW2023Dong create adversarial examples that mislead popular models, such as Bard, by attacking white-box surrogate vision encoders.}
elements.sent-2411-204: Cao~\etal~\cite{ICLRW2024Gao examine adversarial attacks on referring expression comprehension (REC) tasks, where perturbations mislead object identification.}
elements.sent-2411-205: Dou~\etal~\cite{LAMPS2024Dou further refine this by transforming inputs to minimize embedding deviations, making adversarial attacks harder to detect.}
elements.sent-2411-206: Other studies, like Zhang~\etal~\cite{USENIX2024Zhang , add noise to one modality to force its embedding to align with another, deceiving the model by exploiting cross-modal perturbations.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Some research shifts focus to adversarial attacks triggering toxic outputs, \ie jailbreaking.
elements.sent-2411-208: Carlini~\etal~\cite{NeurIPS2023Carlini show how continuous-domain adversarial images can prompt toxic content from language models, bypassing safety mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-209: Qi~\etal~\cite{AAAI2024Qi demonstrate how a single visual adversarial example combined with harmful instructions can jailbreak LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Bagdasaryan~\etal~\cite{arxiv2023Bagdasaryan blend adversarial perturbations into images or audio, which steer the model towards attacker-chosen outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Niu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Niu enhance attack transferability, generating adversarial images that consistently jailbreak MLLMs across different prompts and models, using a maximum likelihood-based approach.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Bailey~\etal~\cite{ICML2024Bailey discuss ``image hijacks'', where adversarial images manipulate model outputs, causing safety and security vulnerabilities like disinformation, context leakage, and jailbreaks.}
elements.sent-2411-213: At a technical level, these attacks show how perturbations in the input space can mislead the model toward various vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-214: An additional focus is increasing prediction latency.
elements.sent-2411-215: Gao~\etal~\cite{ICLR2024Gao introduce verbose images that slow down sequence generation, affecting model efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-216: Baras~\etal~\cite{arxiv2023Baras propose QuantAttack, targeting quantized models by slowing their inference process and increasing memory usage and energy consumption.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Recent studies target both image and text modalities to enhance misleading effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-218: Zhang~\etal~\cite{MM2022Zhang propose Co-Attack, jointly perturbing image and text modalities in vision-language pretrained models (VLPs).}
elements.sent-2411-219: Yang~\etal~\cite{CVPR2024Yang show that combined attacks on text and images can bypass safeguards, like prompt filters and NSFW detectors.}
elements.sent-2411-220: With increased effectiveness of multimodal perturbations, research further examines transferability across models.
elements.sent-2411-221: Wang~\etal~\cite{Oakland2024Wang tailor modality consistency and discrepancy features, focusing perturbations on key image regions while altering relevant text.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Luo~\etal~\cite{ICLR2024Luo optimize cross-prompt adversarial transferability, adjusting perturbations in text and images to maximize impact.}
elements.sent-2411-223: Lu~\etal~\cite{ICCV2023Lu introduce Set-level Guidance Attack (SGA), which maintains adversarial effectiveness across black-box models through alignment-preserving augmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Finally, AnyDoor~\cite{arXiv2024Lu proposes a test-time backdoor attack that adds perturbations to both image and text.}
elements.sent-2411-225: This backdoor effect activates only when perturbations in both modalities meet, creating a backdoor without altering training data.
elements.sent-2411-226: In unimodal perturbations, noise is often generated based on model gradients to create samples that cross decision boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-227: In contrast, multimodal attacks typically focus on disrupting the alignment between different modalities.
elements.sent-2411-228: Since this alignment is often achieved through contrastive learning, it makes the model’s decision boundaries less distinct and more susceptible to exploitation.. \end{takeaway Structure-based attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Structure-based attacks manipulate input format or structure to deceive models.
elements.sent-2411-230: For example, FigStep~\cite{arxiv2023Gong embeds harmful textual content into images using typography to bypass textual safety mechanisms, avoiding direct harmful text input.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Similarly, Zong~\etal~\cite{ICML2024Zong show that simple structural changes, like shuffling answer choices in multiple-choice prompts, mislead models, highlighting their vulnerability to adversarial permutations in answer sets.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Shayegani~\etal~\cite{ICLR2024Shayegani introduce a gradient-based attack that updates images to match embeddings of malicious triggers in the joint embedding space.}
elements.sent-2411-233: This method combines images with generic prompts to perform jailbreaks, using triggers like textual, OCR-based, visual, and combined textual-visual triggers.
elements.sent-2411-234: Ma~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Ma explore embedding harmful content in images and introduce ``Role-play'' into MLLM jailbreaks.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Li~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Li focus on amplifying malicious intent in text prompts (\eg ``conduct the behavior in the image'') using typography and perturbations to enhance attack effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Recent research emphasizes how these attacks can evade safety-alignment mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-237: Samples that appear harmless in a single modality may produce harmful outcomes when combined in a multimodal input, further complicating the development of effective defenses.
elements.sent-2411-238: Without a reasoning mechanism, models cannot perfectly learn modality mappings, so manipulating signals or noise will always reveal breakpoints.. \end{takeaway.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Mislearning attacks target the training phase of multimodal models with the intention of corrupting the model’s learning process.
elements.sent-2411-240: Attackers inject fake, poisoned, or backdoor samples into the training data, causing the model to learn incorrect patterns or behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-241: This can lead to models that not only produce inaccurate predictions but also exhibit hidden vulnerabilities, such as triggering specific behaviors when exposed to particular inputs during inference (\eg backdoors).
elements.sent-2411-242: The attacker’s goal is to alter the model’s internal representations and understanding of the data, resulting in behavior that deviates from the designers' intentions.
elements.sent-2411-243: Figure~\ref{fig_mislearning in Appendix shows an example of mislearning attacks.. Mismatching-based attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Mismatching-based attacks mismatch the modality pairings in the training samples, such as pairing the text ``dog'' with an image of a cat, to force the model to learn incorrect mappings.
elements.sent-2411-245: From an information theory perspective, this reduces the effective signal $S$ to the model by introducing mismatches between modalities.
elements.sent-2411-246: For example, Shan~\cite{Oakland2024Shan demonstrates a simple ``dirty-label'' poisoning attack against diffusion models by exploiting concept sparsity (only a few samples are associated with a specific concept or prompt).}
elements.sent-2411-247: Similarly, Shadowcast~\cite{NeurIPS2024Xu showcases a traditional label attack on VLM models, and both studies propose stronger attacks by adding image perturbations to the poisoned data for increased stealth.}
elements.sent-2411-248: ImgTrojan~\cite{arxiv2024Tao differs from these approaches by focusing specifically on jailbreak attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-249: It uses clean images as trojans, associating them with malicious jailbreak prompts via poisoned training, setting it apart from other backdoor attacks.. Optimized mismatching attacks.
elements.sent-2411-250: These attacks introduce perturbations ($N$) to one or more modalities to push poisoned samples closer to the target concept in latent/alignment space.
elements.sent-2411-251: For instance, Shan~\cite{Oakland2024Shan maximizes poison potency by introducing guided perturbations that push poisoned images toward ``anchor images'' in the feature space.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Shadowcast~\cite{NeurIPS2024Xu leverages VLMs’ text generation capabilities to craft persuasive, rational-sounding narratives, which skew the learned concepts in the model.. Trigger-injected attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Trigger-injected attacks rely on specific patterns within images or text to activate an incorrect output, which inject created fake signal $S$ into the model, thus compromising the model learning.
elements.sent-2411-254: For example, BadT2I~\cite{MM2023Zhai performs backdoor attacks at three levels, \ie pixel, object, and style, targeting vision semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-255: BadDiffusion~\cite{CVPR2023Chou engineers compromised diffusion processes to implant backdoors.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Chen~\etal~\cite{CVPR2023Chen extend this further with TrojDiff, introducing a diffusion process that biases the generative model during training for effective backdoor implantation.}
elements.sent-2411-257: For VLMs, VL-Trojan~\cite{arxiv2024Liang uses an isolating and clustering strategy to enhance image trigger learning, while BadCLIP~\cite{CVPR2024Liang aligns visual trigger patterns with textual semantics using a Bayesian-inspired framework, making it harder to unlearn backdoors.}}
elements.sent-2411-258: Efficient backdoor attacks are also gaining focus.
elements.sent-2411-259: Han~\etal~\cite{Oakland2024Han propose the first data- and computation-efficient multimodal backdoor attacks, highlighting the growing sophistication of backdoor attack strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-260: More critically, trigger patterns in multimodal backdoors can be optimized based on multiple modalities simultaneously or focused solely on the most vulnerable modality, enabling stronger attacks with fewer manipulations.. \end{takeaway.}
elements.sent-2411-261: Inferring attacks focus on extracting private or sensitive information from the model by analyzing its outputs.
elements.sent-2411-262: These attacks exploit the model’s responses to uncover hidden patterns or sensitive data that were used during training or are embedded within the model’s architecture.
elements.sent-2411-263: In multimodal models, such attacks can be particularly harmful due to the diverse and rich nature of the data on which the models are trained.
elements.sent-2411-264: In the context of information theory, the model can be viewed as a communication channel that transmits information learned from the training data, with its output serving as a signal that contains both valuable information and noise.
elements.sent-2411-265: The attacker seeks to maximize their understanding of the private or sensitive data based on the model's outputs.
elements.sent-2411-266: Specifically, the attacker aims to filter out the noise to reverse-engineer the input signal, \ie increasing the signal-to-noise ratio ($S/N$), allowing them to extract more meaningful insights about the private data.. Prompt-based attacks.
elements.sent-2411-267: One of the pioneering works in prompt-based data extraction in MFMs is by Carlini~\etal~\cite{USENIX2023Carlini , who use a generate-and-filter pipeline to extract over a thousand training examples from state-of-the-art models, particularly diffusion models.. Similarity-based inferring attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) have also been explored in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-269: M4I~\cite{NeurIPS2022Hu introduces two methods, metric-based and feature-based attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-270: The feature-based M4I uses a pre-trained shadow multimodal feature extractor to compare input and output in feature space, allowing the attacker to infer whether data was part of the model’s training set.
elements.sent-2411-271: Similarly, Ko~\cite{ICCV2023Ko assumes the attacker can query the model with pairs of images and captions and, based on the cosine similarity between image and text in alignment feature space, identifies membership status.}
elements.sent-2411-272: EncoderMI~\cite{CCS2021Liu focuses on image encoders, analyzing similarities in feature vectors between augmented image samples.}
elements.sent-2411-273: CLiD~\cite{arXiv2024Zhai identifies a conditional overfitting phenomenon in text-to-image diffusion models, where models tend to overfit the conditional distribution of images based on corresponding text, rather than the marginal distribution of images alone, revealing a unique vulnerability in the context of membership inference.. Distribution-based attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Prompt stealing is another form of inferring attack.
elements.sent-2411-275: Yang~\cite{arxiv2024Yang proposes a two-phase process—prompt mutation and prompt pruning, where the attacker gradually infers target prompts by analyzing the critical features of input-output pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-276: Shen~\cite{USENIX2024Shen goes further by considering both the subject and modifiers in prompts to diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-277: A subject generator infers the subject prompt, while a modifier detector deduces the modifier prompts within the generated image.
elements.sent-2411-278: This introduces a new vulnerability for privacy exposure, as attackers can directly exploit the alignment between modalities, rather than relying solely on inferring model outputs.
elements.sent-2411-279: These attacks can be executed more subtly and with greater ease, underscoring the need for stronger regulatory measures to ensure effective oversight and protection.. \end{takeaway.}

elements.sent-2411-280: MFMs are increasingly used to build autonomous multimodal agents for real-world environments.
elements.sent-2411-281: However, recent studies show that these agents introduce new security risks~\cite{IJCAI2024Liu,KDD2024Zhao,arxiv2024Deng,arxiv2024Mo .}
elements.sent-2411-282: In this section, we categorize system-level security attacks into three types based on targeted information flows.
elements.sent-2411-283: The first category, attacks on agent actions, involves manipulating agents' behavior by exploiting limited bandwidth constraints and impairing agents' information processing.
elements.sent-2411-284: The second category, attacks on agent interactions, involves compromising communication bandwidth, causing miscoordination between agents.
elements.sent-2411-285: The third category, attacks on system memory information flows, targets the storage, retrieval, and integrity of data.
elements.sent-2411-286: Several attacks on agent actions exploit vulnerabilities at the model level, such as misleading attacks, to misdirect agents’ behavior in multimodal systems.
elements.sent-2411-287: Wu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Wu2 show how adversarial text strings combined with perturbations of trigger images can deceive multimodal agents.}
elements.sent-2411-288: Similarly, Fu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Fu reveal that obfuscated adversarial prompts can transfer to production-level agents, exposing vulnerabilities in real-world deployments.}
elements.sent-2411-289: ROBOPAIR~\cite{arxiv2024Robey presents an algorithm that jailbreaks LLM-controlled robots, triggering harmful actions by targeting their language models.}
elements.sent-2411-290: Prompt injection attacks represent another form of misdirection, where malicious data is injected into multimodal agents.
elements.sent-2411-291: Perez~\etal~\cite{NeurIPSW2022Perez explore goal hijacking and prompt leaking, where attackers alter model objectives and extract confidential information through manipulated prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-292: Liu~\etal~\cite{USENIX2024Liu2 formalize and benchmark prompt injection techniques, demonstrating their ability to compromise model security.}
elements.sent-2411-293: Pedro~\etal~\cite{arxiv2023Pedro study prompt-to-SQL injections, which lead to malicious SQL code generation, a serious system-wide risk.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Indirect prompt injection attacks let adversaries exploit multimodal models remotely by embedding prompts in data retrieved at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-295: Greshake~\etal~\cite{CCSW2023Greshake show how maliciously embedded prompts can trigger arbitrary code execution, manipulating application functionality.}
elements.sent-2411-296: Wu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Wu4 and WIPI~\cite{arxiv2024Wu5 explore indirect prompt injections that exploit web data to mislead web agents.}}
elements.sent-2411-297: Nakash~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Nakash show that even benign requests can trigger indirect prompt injections, increasing the likelihood of subsequent malicious actions.}
elements.sent-2411-298: Dong~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Dong investigate exploiting low-rank adapters to control LLMs, compromising systems by executing malicious instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-299: These attacks compromise or bypass the bandwidth constraints in information flow, leading to misinformation and breaking the integrity of the system, ultimately causing harmful outcomes.. \end{takeaway.}
elements.sent-2411-300: In this subsection, we explore attacks on agent interactions, focusing on how adversaries can bypass bandwidth constraints to compromise multi-agent systems.
elements.sent-2411-301: Tan~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Tan investigate infectious attacks, where jailbreaking a single agent spreads harmful behaviors to others in the system.}
elements.sent-2411-302: They demonstrate that an MLLM agent, manipulated to generate specific prompts, can ``infect'' other agents in a society of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-303: Similarly, Gu~\etal~\cite{ICML2024Gu simulate multi-agent environments with up to one million LLaVA-1.5 agents, showing that feeding an adversarial image into any agent’s memory can trigger an infectious jailbreak, spreading harm across the system.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Weeks~\etal~\cite{ACSAC2023Weeks explore toxicity injection targeting chatbots, focusing on Dialog-based Learning (DBL), where attackers inject toxic content into a chatbot’s training data, leading to harmful outputs in future interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-305: Yu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Yu and Huang~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Huang investigate topological properties that enhance the safety and resilience of multi-agent systems, showing that malicious agents can introduce subtle errors too stealthy for non-specialized agents to detect, posing significant security risks.}}
elements.sent-2411-306: Prompt infection is another threat, where LLM-to-LLM prompt injection spreads malicious instructions across multi-agent systems, disrupting information flow.

elements.sent-2411-396: To address system-level security issues in practice, we believe that compartmentalization, a concept borrowed from the software domain, could be a potential solution for safeguarding MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-397: In software systems, compartmentalization involves dividing the system into isolated components or modules, each with its own distinct set of permissions and controls.
elements.sent-2411-398: This limits the potential damage from an attack, as the compromise of one compartment does not automatically expose others.
elements.sent-2411-399: Key techniques include restricting access to sensitive resources, enforcing boundaries between processes, and controlling communication pathways to prevent unintended interactions or information leaks.
elements.sent-2411-400: In the context of MFMs, system compartmentalization starts with ensuring the safety of each isolated component at the model level.
elements.sent-2411-401: However, achieving flawless protection at this level is challenging, as shown in previous discussions and our experimental results.
elements.sent-2411-402: Therefore, the key to effective compartmentalization lies in managing the flow of information across the system as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-403: This includes controlling interactions between agents and applications, agents and system memory, and between different agents themselves.
elements.sent-2411-404: Compartmentalization at system level involves implementing access control mechanisms and information transmission constraints on the channel bandwidth that regulate how data moves through the system and who or what can access particular parts of it.
elements.sent-2411-405: In this section, we propose several promising directions for future research on the safety and security of MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-406: Attacks and defenses considering alignment space.
elements.sent-2411-407: Recent studies, including our own experiments, show that attacks targeting multiple modalities simultaneously can be more potent than those focused on a single modality.
elements.sent-2411-408: This is because perturbations across modalities can amplify their effects in the alignment space, lowering the attack power required for each modality.
elements.sent-2411-409: This makes such attacks stealthier and more effective.
elements.sent-2411-410: From an information theory perspective, manipulation across multiple modalities attacks the system's information flow, compromising the integrity and alignment of information between modalities.
elements.sent-2411-411: Future research should focus on understanding how adversarial perturbations propagate through these information flows and across the alignment space.
elements.sent-2411-412: Defenses should consider this space and how attacks spread through information channels, ensuring protection strategies address cross-modal interactions and their combined impact on the final output.
elements.sent-2411-414: The rise of agent-enabled multimodal systems, which integrate complex models like MFMs to perform various tasks, introduces new safety and security risks.
elements.sent-2411-415: The interaction between different types of information flows (input, model, output, agent action, agent interaction, memory), varying channel capacities, and attacker goals creates opportunities for novel attacks not yet fully explored.
elements.sent-2411-416: Adversaries may exploit bottlenecks or vulnerabilities in model alignment to introduce new forms of manipulation or data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-417: Our proposed framework can help identify these new threats by analyzing the interactions between flows and attack vectors, ensuring that vulnerabilities are systematically evaluated.. Holistic defense strategies.
elements.sent-2411-418: Current defense strategies focus on protecting specific model-level vulnerabilities or single modalities, often overlooking broader system-level risks.
elements.sent-2411-419: Protecting multimodal systems effectively requires a comprehensive approach that considers all information flows.
elements.sent-2411-420: This means not only reinforcing model-level defenses but also strengthening the full spectrum of information flow to minimize noise and apply constraints on bandwidth between system components.
elements.sent-2411-421: Balancing these factors is crucial for preventing attacks while maintaining performance.
elements.sent-2411-422: Effective defenses should aim to reduce trade-offs between robustness and performance, ensuring protections in one flow do not weaken another part of the system.
elements.sent-2411-423: Future research should develop integrated defense strategies that optimize security across both model and system levels, ensuring multimodal systems are resilient to various threats.
elements.sent-2411-424: This paper presents our SoK on the cybersafety and cybersecurity of MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-425: By applying an information-theoretic framework, we provide a novel perspective on evaluating and classifying threats, allowing us to unify concepts of model safety and system security in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-426: This offers a deeper understanding of the risks that arise from integrating multiple modalities.
elements.sent-2411-427: We examined existing defense mechanisms and highlighted critical gaps, particularly the lack of protection for cross-modal alignment and the need for more comprehensive, system-level defenses.
elements.sent-2411-428: We believe that future research should focus on addressing these gaps to ensure the secure and reliable deployment of MFMs across various applications.

elements.sent-2411-451: Multimodal foundation models (MFMs) integrate diverse data modalities to support complex and wide-ranging tasks.
elements.sent-2411-452: However, this integration also introduces distinct safety and security challenges.
elements.sent-2411-453: In this paper, we unify the concepts of safety and security in the context of MFMs by identifying critical threats that arise from both model behavior and system-level interactions.
elements.sent-2411-454: We propose a taxonomy grounded in information theory, evaluating risks through the concepts of channel capacity, signal, noise, and bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-455: This perspective provides a principled way to analyze how information flows through MFMs and how vulnerabilities can emerge across modalities.
elements.sent-2411-456: Building on this foundation, we investigate defense mechanisms through the lens of a minimax game between attackers and defenders, highlighting key gaps in current research.
elements.sent-2411-457: In particular, we identify insufficient protection for cross-modal alignment and a lack of systematic and scalable defense strategies.
elements.sent-2411-458: Our work offers both a theoretical and practical foundation for advancing the safety and security of MFMs, supporting the development of more robust and trustworthy systems.
elements.sent-2411-459: Multimodal foundation models (MFMs) are a form of artificial intelligence (AI) which integrate diverse data types, such as text, images, and audio, into unified frameworks capable of adapting to numerous downstream tasks~\cite{fei2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-460: Notable examples include the feature alignment model OpenAI's CLIP~\cite{radford2021learning , image-generating diffusion models~\cite{rombach2022high , and multimodal large language models such as GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-461: However, the rapid integration of MFMs into high-stakes environments, including autonomous vehicles and medical diagnostics, amplifies the need for robust safety and security measures~\cite{KDD2024Zhao,han2022multimodal .}
elements.sent-2411-462: In particular there is a need to (1) ensure reliable, harm-free performance (safety), and (2) protect against malicious attacks (security).
elements.sent-2411-463: Attacks on MFMs often undermine both safety and security, highlighting the need for unified analysis, particularly as the richer data forms processed by MFMs have led to the emergence of a broader range of more complex and covert attack vectors and scenarios compared to simpler unimodal models.
elements.sent-2411-464: This motivates our Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) paper.

We propose an information-theoretic framework that unifies safety and security in MFMs, uses it to categorize threats at both the model and system levels, and analyze defenses as a minimax game between attackers and defenders, revealing critical gaps in current research.} \label{fig_overview}
elements.sent-2411-465: To address the challenge of unifying safety and security in MFMs, we propose a novel framework grounded in information theory.
elements.sent-2411-466: Information theory provides a principled foundation for analyzing how information is transmitted, processed, and integrated across multiple modalities.
elements.sent-2411-467: Specifically, we adapt concepts from the Shannon Hartley theorem~\cite{taub1986principles,ziemer2014principles , including channel capacity, bandwidth, signal, and noise, as an interpretive abstraction for reasoning about how information flow is disrupted in MFMs.}
elements.sent-2411-468: This abstraction allows us to unify threat analysis under a common information theoretic lens.
elements.sent-2411-469: At the model level, we focus on safety threats that reduce the quality of the input signal or amplify the disruptive effects of noise, thereby degrading the model's internal reasoning and output reliability.
elements.sent-2411-470: At the system level, we extend this analysis by considering bandwidth constraints and examining how information flows between agents and components, revealing how broader system interactions can introduce or intensify risks.
elements.sent-2411-471: Furthermore, we analyze existing defense mechanisms by framing the interaction between attackers and defenders as a deterministic minimax game, which models how adversaries attempt to maximize damage while defenders attempt to minimize it.
elements.sent-2411-472: We empirically solve the minimax problem to assess the effectiveness of current defenses and identify gaps in their ability to protect multimodal systems.
elements.sent-2411-473: Based on these insights, we outline future research directions to enhance the resilience of MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-474: An overview of our systematization is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig_overview .}
elements.sent-2411-475: Compared with other related surveys and SoKs, our study offers a unified view of safety and security across both model and system levels for MFMs.

elements.sent-2411-485: We provide a comprehensive review of \nPapersRemain recent works on the safety and security of MFMs, highlighting how diverse modalities and complex information flows introduce unique vulnerabilities across both model and system levels.
elements.sent-2411-486: We introduce a novel threat taxonomy based on information theory, adapting the Shannon–Hartley theorem~\cite{taub1986principles,ziemer2014principles to model how information flows from inputs to outputs in multimodal learning.}
elements.sent-2411-487: This framework unifies safety analysis (centered on signal-to-noise ratio) and security analysis (focused on bandwidth constraints).
elements.sent-2411-488: We identify 6 key information flows: prediction, learning, and inference at the model level, and interactions among agents, with applications, and with system memory at the system level.
elements.sent-2411-489: These allow us to categorize attacks according to the specific information flows they target.
elements.sent-2411-490: Based on this, we classify \nModelAttacks model-level safety attacks and \nSystemAttacks system-level security attacks, revealing that cross-modal attacks can often be executed more subtly and effectively.
elements.sent-2411-491: We analyze current MFM defenses through a deterministic minimax game between attackers and defenders, supported by empirically evaluating \nDefenses defenses.
elements.sent-2411-492: Our results show that model-level defenses can mitigate some threats but are often limited and costly, achieving no more than 51.8\% accuracy on adversarial examples under co-modality misleading attacks and performing poorly against mislearning attacks.
elements.sent-2411-493: In contrast, system-level safety filters show stronger potential.
elements.sent-2411-494: These findings suggest that model-only defenses are insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-495: We outline several directions for future research, emphasizing the need for better understanding of system-level interactions and deeper investigation into cross-modal vulnerabilities in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-496: Our study proposes a conceptual framework for identifying and explaining safety and security threats in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-497: Instead of building automated tools for large-scale attack or vulnerability detection, our SoK work emphasizes the theoretical foundations needed to analyze how these threats emerge and impact the information flow within and across multimodal systems.. Key findings.
elements.sent-2411-498: The architecture of MFMs, particularly their feature alignment and aggregation components, makes them susceptible to subtle model-level attacks.
elements.sent-2411-499: Existing defenses often address isolated information flows or individual modalities, leaving gaps in protection.
elements.sent-2411-500: We find that enforcing bandwidth constraints at the system level can offer stronger safeguards, while relying solely on model-level defenses is often insufficient and may fail under complex, cross-modal attacks.
elements.sent-2411-501: In unimodal learning, models operate within a discrete feature space, extracting patterns from a single data type by converting inputs into vectors and mapping them to output labels.
elements.sent-2411-502: In contrast, multimodal learning (Figure~\ref{fig_single_multi in Appendix) integrates continuous feature spaces from different modalities by projecting them into a shared alignment space.}
elements.sent-2411-503: Rather than mapping each modality directly to outputs, multimodal models learn unified representations that link diverse data types.
elements.sent-2411-504: This alignment enables richer understanding and more complex capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-505: However, the inclusion of diverse information and the process of aligning feature spaces significantly increase the attack surface, making it more difficult to mitigate security and safety risks.

elements.sent-2411-511: In this section, we first outline the safety and security objectives in MFMs, then adapt information theory to analyze how these concepts interact.
elements.sent-2411-512: In domains such as aviation, nuclear energy, chemistry, power systems, and information technology, safety and security are traditionally distinguished by intentionality~\cite{pietre2010sema,hansson2013ethics,ale2009risk,firesmith2003common,porzsolt2011safety .}
elements.sent-2411-513: Safety concerns the prevention of unintentional failures or harm, while security focuses on protection against deliberate malicious actions\cite{arxiv2024Qi .}
elements.sent-2411-514: In the context of multimodal learning, safety refers to a model’s ability to function reliably and without causing harm, even in the presence of unexpected inputs.
elements.sent-2411-515: Security, by contrast, addresses the system’s robustness against adversarial attacks and malicious exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-517: While safety and security are often distinguished by the presence of malicious intent, this distinction can be misleading in the context of security research where most threat models already assume adversarial intent, making it problematic to categorize threats based solely on intent.
elements.sent-2411-518: For instance, attacks such as jailbreaks~\cite{NeurIPS2023Carlini,AAAI2024Qi and latency-based exploits~\cite{ICLR2024Gao,arxiv2023Baras are typically labeled as safety issues, yet they clearly involve deliberate adversarial behavior and should be treated as security concerns.}}
elements.sent-2411-519: Moreover, the scope of model safety includes goals such as adversarial robustness~\cite{NDSS2018Xu,ICLR2018Guo , uncertainty quantification, trojan detection~\cite{arxiv2023Zhang2 , and value alignment~\cite{amodei2016concrete , many of which are directly linked to the same root causes exploited in security breaches.}}}
elements.sent-2411-520: We argue that at the model level, threats are most accurately understood as exploiting failures in model safety mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-521: Therefore, model-level threats should be regarded as manifestations of safety failures.
elements.sent-2411-522: AI agents are increasingly integrated into broader software ecosystems such as web platforms, email systems, and file management services.
elements.sent-2411-523: At this system level, security threats emerge that cannot be fully understood or mitigated by focusing only on the model.
elements.sent-2411-524: For example, indirect prompt injection attacks~\cite{CCSW2023Greshake,arxiv2024Wu can insert malicious prompts into external sources retrieved during inference, such as emails or web pages~\cite{arxiv2024Wu5,arxiv2024Wu .}}
elements.sent-2411-525: These attacks may cause the AI agent to unknowingly execute harmful instructions, potentially leading to information leakage or unauthorized actions.
elements.sent-2411-526: Such risks can occur even when each system component demonstrates good safety properties.
elements.sent-2411-527: Without system-level threat detection and operational constraints, serious breaches may still happen.
elements.sent-2411-528: Therefore, we emphasize that security threats should be addressed at the system level, where interactions among agents, applications, and shared memory introduce new risks and vulnerabilities.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Unifying Security and Safety in MFMs
elements.sent-2411-529: A machine learning model can be conceptualized as a channel for information transmission, where input data flows through the model and generates outputs that may influence other components in a broader system.
elements.sent-2411-530: From this perspective, information theory offers a rigorous foundation for analyzing how information is transmitted, processed, and fused in MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-531: Specifically, we adapt the Shannon–Hartley theorem~\cite{taub1986principles,ziemer2014principles , which quantifies the maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a communication channel (\eg the model) in the presence of noise (\eg threats), for analyzing multimodal safety and security: \begin{equation}\label{eq_sh_theorem} C=B\log_2 (1+{S}/{N}),\end{equation} where $C$ is the channel capacity (the measure of information throughput), $B$ is the bandwidth of the channel (the transmission capacity), $S$ is the signal power (the meaningful information), $N$ is the noise power (the disruptive or irrelevant information).}
elements.sent-2411-532: In the context of multimodal models, we can adapt these definitions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-533: Channel capacity represents the model's ability to effectively learn from and predict based on inputs.
elements.sent-2411-534: It reflects how well the model transmits meaningful information.
elements.sent-2411-535: Signal refers to the meaningful information that a model can extract or interpret from input samples, including semantics or concepts in a sample across modalities, such as textual features in documents, visual patterns in images, or auditory cues in sound clips.
elements.sent-2411-536: The structure in which this information is presented, such as natural language text or its equivalent representation embedded in an image, can also signal clarity and strength.
elements.sent-2411-537: Noise includes all forms of irrelevant or disruptive information that can distort the intended signal.
elements.sent-2411-538: It can originate from sensor errors, data inconsistencies, or adversarial perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-539: Noise can be external, coming from misleading or irrelevant inputs, or internal, arising from model uncertainty or inherent stochasticity in decision making.
elements.sent-2411-540: Bandwidth describes the capacity of a system or agent to transmit meaningful information.
elements.sent-2411-541: It depends on the communication constraints among components and governs how efficiently information can be shared or acted upon.
elements.sent-2411-542: Bandwidth plays a key role in shaping both performance and responsiveness across the system.
elements.sent-2411-543: Robust models must accurately interpret and integrate information across modalities to avoid errors or harmful outputs.
elements.sent-2411-544: Reducing noise, whether through data preprocessing, feature selection, or other methods, helps preserve relevant information while minimizing disruptive factors, thereby enhancing both performance and safety.
elements.sent-2411-545: From an information-theoretic perspective, given Equation~\ref{eq_sh_theorem , safety-focused approaches aim to increase the signal-to-noise ratio ($S/N$).}
elements.sent-2411-546: In contrast, model-level attacks degrade this ratio by introducing noise or distorting the signal, either during training or inference, undermining a model’s ability to interpret inputs correctly.
elements.sent-2411-547: Bandwidth~$B$ in Equation~\ref{eq_sh_theorem influences the flow of information between different applications and agents, directly affecting the system’s ability to coordinate and respond effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-548: Prior work~\cite{arxiv2024Wu has shown that even when an individual agent maintains strong model-level controls (\ie a high $S/N$), attackers can exploit cross-agent interactions to trigger significant security breaches.}
elements.sent-2411-549: These vulnerabilities often arise from insufficient system-level management of bandwidth, such as unrestricted agent actions or unfiltered information exchange.
elements.sent-2411-550: To mitigate such risks, it is critical to implement system-level bandwidth constraints, including restrictions on agent behavior and filters that control the flow of information between agents.
elements.sent-2411-551: In this section, we introduce model- and system-level threats within a unified threat model, categorizing them based on the adversary’s goals, knowledge, and capabilities, and further analyze the information flows within MFMs to identify key vulnerability points that can be exploited.
elements.sent-2411-552: At the model level, adversaries seek to disrupt model performance or extract sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-553: Common goals include generating adversarial examples~\cite{NeurIPS2023Yin,ICCVW2023Schlarmann , poisoning training data~\cite{Oakland2024Shan,NeurIPS2024Xu , inserting backdoors~\cite{arXiv2024Lu,MM2023Zhai , jailbreaking~\cite{NeurIPS2023Carlini,AAAI2024Qi , increasing latency or energy use~\cite{ICLR2024Gao,arxiv2023Baras , stealing prompts~\cite{arxiv2024Yang,USENIX2024Shen , extracting private data~\cite{USENIX2023Carlini , and inferring membership~\cite{NeurIPS2022Hu,ICCV2023Ko .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-554: At the system level, attackers target the broader infrastructure to induce unintended or harmful behavior.
elements.sent-2411-555: Their goals include manipulating outputs~\cite{arxiv2024Wu2,arxiv2024Mo , hijacking task objectives~\cite{USENIX2024Liu2,NeurIPSW2022Perez , triggering malicious payloads~\cite{arxiv2024Wu5,arxiv2024Wu , injecting harmful code~\cite{arxiv2023Pedro , spreading disinformation~\cite{USENIX2025Zou,EMNLP2023Zhong , or leaking prompts~\cite{NeurIPSW2022Perez .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-556: Notably, some threats, such as backdoors, jailbreaks, and data leakage, may originate at the model level but be exploited at the system level.
elements.sent-2411-557: More details regarding adversary's goals and knowledge are provided in Appendix~\ref{sec_adversary_goals .}
elements.sent-2411-558: From an information flow perspective, all these adversary goals, whether at the model or system level, aim to degrade the effective channel capacity.
elements.sent-2411-559: By manipulating the flow of information, attackers reduce the model's ability to produce accurate or authorized outputs.
elements.sent-2411-560: Attacks such as adversarial examples and data poisoning compromise the integrity of the signal, while backdoors and jailbreaks undermine alignment and reliability of the information channel.
elements.sent-2411-561: At the system level, manipulations such as goal hijacking and malicious payloads aim to corrupt the flow of information across the system, further limiting the capacity of the communication channel and compromising security and safety.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figs/information_flow.pdf} \caption{An illustration of information flows in MFM system (represented by arrows).} \label{fig_information_flow}
elements.sent-2411-562: Multimodal learning presents distinct challenges and vulnerabilities compared to unimodal systems, stemming from both internal model flows and external system interactions, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig_information_flow .}
elements.sent-2411-563: At the model level, the information flows include: Prediction information flow (\eg~\cite{MM2022Zhang,NeurIPS2023Yin ) involves processing multimodal inputs through the model to produce outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-564: Adversaries may introduce malicious inputs to misleading the model, causing inaccurate or biased results.
elements.sent-2411-565: Learning information flow (\eg~\cite{Oakland2024Shan ) concerns training or fine-tuning with input data; attackers may poison the training set, causing the model to mislearn and resulting in incorrect predictions or behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-566: Inference information flow (\eg~\cite{ICCV2023Ko,USENIX2023Carlini ) refers to scenarios where adversaries reverse-engineer outputs or use crafted queries to extract private or sensitive information from the model training set.}
elements.sent-2411-567: From the system perspective, the information flows between various components, such as models, databases, and applications.
elements.sent-2411-568: Action information flow between agents and applications (\eg~\cite{NeurIPSW2022Perez,arxiv2023Pedro ) governs how agents perform actions based on model outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-569: Interaction information flow between multi-agents (\eg~\cite{arxiv2024Tan, ACSAC2023Weeks ) involves inter-agent communication and coordination, which can be exploited by adversaries by injecting false information that propagates across the system.}
elements.sent-2411-570: Memory information flow between agent and system memory (\eg~\cite{EMNLP2023Zhong, arxiv2024Zeng3 ) refers to how agents store, retrieve, and rely on historical data for decision-making.}
elements.sent-2411-571: Attackers may tamper with memory content to alter agent decision-making over time.. Our taxonomy.
elements.sent-2411-572: Traditionally, safety risks and security attacks have been categorized based on attack outcomes, such as adversarial examples or jailbreaks, rather than identifying the underlying vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-573: To offer clearer insights into how the learning process is exploited, we propose a taxonomy based on targeted information flows.
elements.sent-2411-574: Model-level threats compromise prediction, learning, or inference flows, while system-level threats exploit flows tied to agent actions, interactions, and memory.
elements.sent-2411-575: Thus unified, information flow-based perspective clarifies how multimodal systems are exposed to both safety and security risks.
