elements.sent-2411-576: Model-level safety threats in MFMs can be grouped into three categories based on the vulnerable information flows they target.
elements.sent-2411-577: This section explores each: input misleading, model mislearning, and output inference.
elements.sent-2411-578: This taxonomy is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig_tax_safety , and related works are summarized in Table~\ref{tab_big_table .}}

elements.sent-2411-579: Misleading attacks aim to deceive multimodal models during inference, causing them to produce incorrect or manipulated outputs.
elements.sent-2411-580: These attacks exploit the model’s vulnerabilities by introducing carefully crafted inputs that mislead its decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-581: Techniques include adversarial examples, prompt manipulations, or subtle alterations to input data that distort how multimodal content is interpreted.
elements.sent-2411-582: Recent work has identified two major types of misleading attacks: perturbation-based and structure-based.
elements.sent-2411-583: From an information theory perspective, perturbation-based attacks increase noise in the system ($N\uparrow$), which degrades channel capacity and weakens model performance by distorting the input signal.
elements.sent-2411-584: Structure-based attacks instead alter the signal itself ($S\downarrow$), e.g. embedding text within images.
elements.sent-2411-585: By manipulating modality-specific features, inconsistencies in how models handle different types of input can be exploited.
elements.sent-2411-586: A model might be more responsive to harmful content in text than in images, even when both carry the same message.
elements.sent-2411-587: In such cases, the signal recognized through the image modality may be reduced, further lowering the channel capacity.
elements.sent-2411-588: Such attacks are usually subtle, aiming to mislead while evading detection.. Perturbation-based attacks.
elements.sent-2411-589: These attacks aim to induce incorrect predictions by exploiting weaknesses in a model.
elements.sent-2411-590: For example, VLAttack~\cite{NeurIPS2023Yin perturbs images and, if unsuccessful, switches to text, leveraging pre-trained vision-language models to improve cross-modal adversarial transfer.}
elements.sent-2411-591: Schlarmann~\etal~\cite{ICCVW2023Schlarmann design white-box image perturbations to manipulate model-generated captions.}
elements.sent-2411-592: Dong~\etal~\cite{NeurIPSW2023Dong attack surrogate white-box vision encoders to mislead models such as Bard.}
elements.sent-2411-593: Cao~\etal~\cite{ICLRW2024Gao perturb referring expression comprehension (REC) inputs to mislead object identification, while Dou~\etal~\cite{LAMPS2024Dou enhance stealth by minimizing embedding deviations.}}
elements.sent-2411-594: Some research explores how adversarial attacks trigger toxic outputs, \ie jailbreaking.
elements.sent-2411-595: Carlini~\etal~\cite{NeurIPS2023Carlini show how continuous-domain adversarial images can prompt toxic content from language models.}
elements.sent-2411-596: Bagdasaryan~\etal~\cite{arxiv2023Bagdasaryan blend adversarial perturbations into images or audio to induce attacker-specified outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-597: Niu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Niu enhance attack transferability by generating adversarial images that consistently jailbreak MLLMs across prompts and models, using a maximum likelihood-based approach.}
elements.sent-2411-598: Bailey~\etal~\cite{ICML2024Bailey propose ``image hijacks'' that lead to safety failures like disinformation, context leakage, and jailbreaks.}
elements.sent-2411-599: Gao~\etal~\cite{ICLR2024Gao craft verbose images to slow sequence generation.}

elements.sent-2411-655: We categorize system-level security attacks into three types based on targeted information flows.
elements.sent-2411-656: Several attacks on agent actions exploit vulnerabilities at the model level, such as misleading attacks, to misdirect agent behavior in multimodal systems.
elements.sent-2411-657: Wu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Wu2 demonstrate how adversarial text combined with perturbed trigger images can deceive multimodal agents.}
elements.sent-2411-658: Similarly, Fu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Fu reveal that obfuscated adversarial prompts transfer to production agents, exposing real-world weaknesses.}
elements.sent-2411-659: Prompt injection attacks are another form of misdirection, where malicious data is injected into multimodal agents.
elements.sent-2411-660: Perez~\etal~\cite{NeurIPSW2022Perez investigate goal hijacking and prompt leaking, where attackers manipulate model objectives and extract confidential information.}
elements.sent-2411-661: Pedro~\etal~\cite{arxiv2023Pedro study prompt-to-SQL injections, which can generate malicious SQL code and pose serious system-wide risks.}
elements.sent-2411-662: Indirect prompt injection attacks allow adversaries to exploit multimodal models remotely by embedding prompts in data retrieved at inference.
elements.sent-2411-663: Liu~\etal~\cite{USENIX2024Liu2 formalize and benchmark indirect prompt injection techniques, showing how they undermine model security.}
elements.sent-2411-664: Greshake~\etal~\cite{CCSW2023Greshake show how malicious prompts can trigger arbitrary code execution, manipulating application behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-665: Wu~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Wu and WIPI~\cite{arxiv2024Wu5 explore indirect prompt injections using web data to mislead agents.}}
elements.sent-2411-666: Nakash~\etal~\cite{arxiv2024Nakash demonstrate that even benign requests can trigger indirect injections, increasing the risk of subsequent malicious actions.}

elements.sent-2411-679: In many commercial multi-agent systems, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) acts as external memory for MFMs, enabling real-time access to up-to-date information by retrieving relevant data from continuously updated databases.
elements.sent-2411-680: This improves information flow between inputs, memory, and outputs, making generative models more practical and accurate.
elements.sent-2411-681: However, RAG also introduces vulnerabilities, as adversaries can target memory to inject disinformation or bypass bandwidth constraints, compromising the reliability and security of multimodal agents.
elements.sent-2411-682: Attacks on RAG memory often use poisoning techniques to mislead the system and generate incorrect or attacker-aligned responses.

elements.sent-2411-685: HijackRAG~\cite{arxiv2024Zhang3 employs a similar method, while GARAG~\cite{arxiv2024Cho uses a genetic algorithm to simulate retrieval errors like typos and disrupt performance.}}

elements.sent-2411-693: We evaluate defenses through the lens of minimax game theory, where an attacker seeks to maximize the effectiveness of their manipulation (\eg by increasing noise or reducing signal fidelity), and a defender aims to minimize this impact through appropriate countermeasures (\eg noise suppression, signal enhancement, or information flow restoration).
elements.sent-2411-694: At the model level, we formulate the attacker–defender interaction as a two-player deterministic minimax game.
elements.sent-2411-695: We assume that both players operate with full knowledge of the environment, complete observability of each other's strategy spaces.
elements.sent-2411-696: This determinism allows both sides to reason about worst-case scenarios: the attacker can identify the weakest point in the defense, while the defender can anticipate the most damaging attack and optimize their strategy accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-697: By abstracting away randomness, the deterministic minimax framework provides a clean, principled foundation for analyzing adversarial robustness and optimizing defensive strategies under the assumption of fully rational and strategic opponents.
elements.sent-2411-698: Taking detection as an exemplar defender strategy, the attacker selects a strategy $a \in A$ to generate an attack example $x_a' = a(x_a, n, s)$ from a selected original sample $x_a \in X_a$, by manipulating the noise $n$ and/or signal $s$ across one or more modalities.
elements.sent-2411-699: The attacker’s goal is twofold: (1) evade detection by a detector $d(\cdot)$, and (2) mislead the MFM model $m(\cdot)$ into making incorrect predictions.
elements.sent-2411-700: This can be formalized as the following optimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{equation_max} \max_{a \in A} \left[ -\ell(y_c, d(x_a')) - \alpha \cdot \ell(y_a, m(x_a')) \right],\end{equation} where $x_a' = a(x_a, n, s)$, $y_c$ is the correct (clean) label, $y_a$ is the attack target label, and $\ell(\cdot,\cdot)$ denotes a loss function (\eg cross-entropy).
elements.sent-2411-701: The first term maximizes the detector's error to promote evasion, while the second term encourages misclassification by increasing the MFM model's loss with respect to the attack target.
elements.sent-2411-702: The hyperparameter $\alpha$ controls the tradeoff between these objectives.
elements.sent-2411-703: On the defender’s side, the objective is to minimize both false negatives (failing to detect attacks) and false positives (incorrectly flagging clean inputs).
elements.sent-2411-704: Given a set of attack samples $X_a$ and clean samples $X_c$, the defender seeks a strategy $d \in D$ that minimizes the following loss: \begin{equation}\label{equation_min} \min_{d \in D} \left[ \frac{1}{|X_a|} \sum_{x_a \in X_a} \ell(y_a, d(x_a')) + \beta \cdot \frac{1}{|X_c|} \sum_{x_c \in X_c} \ell(y_c, d(x_c)) \right],\end{equation} where $\beta$ adjusts the importance of reducing false positives.
elements.sent-2411-705: The first term penalizes misclassification of adversarial examples as benign, and the second penalizes clean samples being flagged as malicious.

elements.sent-2411-807: In this section, we outline the gaps our SoK has found as several promising avenues for advancing the safety and security of MFMs.
elements.sent-2411-808: Security in agent enabled multimodal systems.
elements.sent-2411-809: Agent-enabled MFMs integrate foundation models with interactive modules such as memory and action components, introducing complex security risks.
elements.sent-2411-810: These systems involve intricate flows between inputs, outputs, actions, and memory, creating new attack surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-811: Adversaries may exploit alignment gaps or bottlenecks to manipulate behavior or leak data.
elements.sent-2411-812: Future work should model these flows systematically to identify vulnerabilities and design dynamic, context-aware defenses.
elements.sent-2411-813: Formal verification of MFM system constraints.
elements.sent-2411-814: To move beyond heuristic protections, future research should pursue formal verification of safety constraints in MFM systems.
elements.sent-2411-815: This includes mathematically proving bounded behaviors and using proof-carrying code to ensure any change to the system preserves these guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-816: Such methods would enable verifiable containment mechanisms and build trust in MFM safety.. Cryptographic control layers.
elements.sent-2411-817: Embedding cryptographic safeguards into MFM systems can enforce human oversight over critical operations.
elements.sent-2411-818: Techniques like secret sharing, threshold approvals, and secure multi-party computation can prevent unauthorized replication, privilege escalation, or code deployment.
elements.sent-2411-819: These mechanisms support distributed, tamper-resistant governance over AGI capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-820: Cross modal attacks and alignment space defenses.
elements.sent-2411-821: Attacks targeting multiple modalities can exploit vulnerabilities in the alignment space, the shared representation across modalities, amplifying effects with minimal perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-822: From an information-theoretic view, these attacks compromise signal coherence.
